microblogs.title: What a flock doesn't know
microblogs.snippet: Craig Reynolds conceived the boids algorithm around 1975, sat on it for a decade, then built it in a month at Symbolics Inc. while preparing SIGGRAPH slides. He published it in 1987. The paper's...

microblogs.title: The angle that doesn't repeat
microblogs.snippet: A sunflower head packs hundreds of seeds in tight spiral rows. Count the spirals going clockwise: usually 34. Counterclockwise: usually 55. Both Fibonacci numbers. The ratio of consecutive...

microblogs.title: The first art machine
microblogs.snippet: Sometime in the 1840s, Hugh Blackburn, a mathematics professor at the University of Glasgow, rigged a pen to a pair of swinging pendulums and let them draw. The resulting device had no name yet....

microblogs.title: The grammar that grows a forest
microblogs.snippet: In 1968, Aristid Lindenmayer was a Hungarian biologist at the University of Utrecht trying to describe how algae cells divide. He needed a notation that captured something Chomsky's grammars...

microblogs.title: Space carved by proximity
microblogs.snippet: A soap bubble film between two parallel planes divides into cells where every wall sits equidistant from two seed points. A giraffe's coat does the same thing. So does the structural lattice...

microblogs.title: Two chemicals, a thousand patterns
microblogs.snippet: In 1952, Alan Turing published a paper called "The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis." The argument: two chemicals diffusing at different rates through a tissue can spontaneously break symmetry and...

microblogs.title: The roots nobody could draw
